summary: Julia Evans addresses the recurring experience of being misgendered in online comments about her technical blog posts, where readers default to 'he' when discussing her work. She reflects on how she never internalized the idea that programming was a male domain, finding the assumption that technical content must be written by a man simply silly. She asserts her sense of belonging in the programming community and pushes back against gatekeeping of all kinds. The post concludes with a broad, inclusive declaration that programming belongs to anyone who writes programs.
key_points: Online commenters consistently default to male pronouns when discussing her technical writing, revealing deep assumptions about who produces technical content
Evans rejects the premise that programming is a male domain, noting she never grew up believing she was worse at math or programming than men around her
Gatekeeping in tech is framed as an illegitimate power grab rather than a natural state of affairs
Programming community membership should be defined by the act of programming itself, not by identity, background, or credentials
money_quotes: I didn't grow up with the idea that I was worse at math or programming than the men around me (because, well, I wasn't!)
I feel like the community belongs to me, and like I'm a part of it.
And when people who have more power push marginalized people out of tech, I think, who do you think you are?
Why do you think this belongs to you, and that you have the right to say who can come and who can't?

Programming doesn't belong to men, or to people who went to MIT, or to white people, or to English-speakers, or to Linux users, or to C programmers, or to people with CS degrees, or to people who are self-taught, or to people who can see, or to people who had easy access to computers when they were young.
If you write programs, it belongs to you.
key_insight: The programming community belongs to everyone who programs, and the default assumption that technical work is produced by men reveals an illegitimate sense of ownership over who gets to participate.
